title: Ethical Decision-Making
simple_html:
Knowing ethical theories is not the same as acting ethically. Ethical decision-making is about the actual mental steps a person goes through when facing a moral choice at work, and why good people sometimes still do the wrong thing.

The most useful map here is James Rest's four-component model. To act ethically you must succeed at all four stages: (1) Moral awareness — noticing that a situation even has an ethical dimension; (2) Moral judgment — deciding which option is right; (3) Moral motivation — caring enough to prioritize the ethical choice over money, career, or convenience; and (4) Moral character — having the courage and follow-through to actually do it. A failure at any one stage produces unethical behavior, even if the other three are strong.

Two things make a situation more likely to be "seen" as ethical. Moral intensity (Jones) means the bigger and closer the harm, the more obvious the ethics. A second danger is moral disengagement (Bandura) — the mental tricks we use to silence our conscience, like "everyone does it" or "it's just business."

Key takeaways

  • Ethical action requires four steps, not one — awareness, judgment, motivation, character.
  • Most workplace wrongdoing starts with a failure of awareness: people don't even notice the issue.
  • Moral intensity explains why distant, diffuse harms (e.g., pollution) get ignored.
  • Moral disengagement lets ordinary people rationalize harm.
  • Use a simple decision checklist to slow down and surface the ethical dimension.
intermediate_html:
This lesson shifts from what is right (the normative theories) to how people actually decide — the descriptive psychology of ethics and the practical tools for making better choices. The goal is to understand why intelligent, well-meaning professionals make unethical decisions and how a structured process can prevent it.

Rest's four-component model

James Rest (1986) argued moral behavior is not a single act but a sequence of four psychological processes, each necessary and none sufficient alone:

  1. Moral awareness (sensitivity) — recognizing that a situation contains a moral issue and that one's actions affect others.
  2. Moral judgment — reasoning about which course of action is most justifiable (this is where the normative frameworks live).
  3. Moral motivation (intent) — prioritizing moral values above competing values such as profit, status, or self-interest.
  4. Moral character (action) — possessing the courage, persistence, and ego strength to carry the choice through under pressure.

The model's diagnostic power is that it locates where a person failed. An employee who stays silent about fraud may have had perfect judgment (component 2) but lacked the courage to act (component 4). A manager who genuinely never noticed a conflict of interest failed at component 1.

Moral intensity — why some issues register and others don't

Thomas Jones (1991) showed that moral awareness and judgment are issue-contingent: they depend on the characteristics of the situation. His six components of moral intensity are: magnitude of consequences, social consensus (how much agreement there is that the act is wrong), probability of effect, temporal immediacy, proximity (social/physical/cultural closeness to victims), and concentration of effect. High-intensity issues (a clear, large, immediate harm to people like us) are easy to spot; low-intensity issues (small harms, spread thin, far away, in the future) slip past our radar — which is precisely why environmental and supply-chain harms are chronically under-addressed.

Moral disengagement — switching off the conscience

Albert Bandura explained how ordinary people commit harmful acts without guilt through moral disengagement: cognitive mechanisms that decouple conduct from self-sanction. Common ones include moral justification ("we did it to save jobs"), euphemistic labeling ("rightsizing," "creative accounting"), displacement of responsibility ("I was just following orders"), diffusion of responsibility ("the whole team signed off"), and dehumanization of victims.

Worked mini-case: applying a decision process

Suppose a procurement manager is offered a lavish "gift" by a supplier just before a contract decision. A structured process: (1) Gather facts — value of the gift, company policy, supplier's intent. (2) Identify the ethical issue — this is a potential conflict of interest and possible bribery (awareness). (3) Identify stakeholders — employer, other bidders, shareholders, the manager. (4) Apply multiple lenses — utilitarian (does this distort the market?), deontological (does accepting violate the duty of fair dealing?), virtue (would an honest professional accept?). (5) Test it — the publicity/front-page test and the role-reversal test. (6) Decide and act — decline, disclose, and document.

How to apply it

In practice: build awareness into systems (ethics training, decision checklists, "ethics by design"), reduce the temptation that fuels disengagement (clear policies, tone at the top), and protect component 4 by creating safe channels — speak-up lines and whistleblower protection — so that courage does not require career sacrifice.
full_html:
The normative theories (utilitarianism, deontology, virtue, justice) answer what ought I to do? This lesson addresses a different and equally important question: how do real people, in real organizations, actually arrive at ethical and unethical choices? This is the domain of descriptive ethics and behavioral ethics, and it is where most corporate scandals are explained — not by a shortage of moral theory, but by failures of perception, motivation, and courage under organizational pressure.

Rest's four-component model of moral behavior

The organizing framework is James Rest's (1986) four-component model, developed within the neo-Kohlbergian tradition. Rest argued that producing moral behavior requires four distinct psychological processes, each a potential point of failure:

  1. Moral sensitivity (awareness) — interpreting a situation as involving a moral issue and recognizing how one's actions affect others' welfare.
  2. Moral judgment — determining which available action is morally justifiable. Lawrence Kohlberg's (1969) stages of moral development describe how this reasoning matures from pre-conventional (self-interest, avoiding punishment), through conventional (conformity, law-and-order), to post-conventional (social contract, universal principles).
  3. Moral motivation — committing to the moral option over competing values; this depends on values prioritization and identity.
  4. Moral character (implementation) — executing the choice despite obstacles, requiring ego strength, perseverance, and courage.

Because the components are sequential and each is necessary, the model is fundamentally diagnostic: it lets us ask at which stage did this person or organization fail? The Challenger disaster, for instance, is often read as a failure of moral motivation and character among engineers who recognized the O-ring risk (judgment intact) but were silenced by organizational pressure.

Issue-contingency: moral intensity

Thomas Jones (1991) extended Rest with a crucial insight: ethical decision-making is issue-contingent. The same person will exhibit different awareness and judgment depending on the moral intensity of the issue, comprising six dimensions: (1) magnitude of consequences, (2) social consensus, (3) probability of effect, (4) temporal immediacy, (5) proximity, and (6) concentration of effect. The practical upshot is that diffuse, delayed, statistically uncertain harms — climate impact, distant-factory labor conditions, data-privacy erosion — score low on intensity and therefore fail to trigger moral awareness in the first place, even among conscientious managers. This connects directly to Lesson 8's treatment of globalization and supply-chain ethics, where distance and diffusion are structural.

Behavioral ethics: bounded ethicality and disengagement

The behavioral ethics movement (Bazerman & Tenbrunsel, 2011) shows that people are boundedly ethical: systematic cognitive biases cause us to act against our own moral standards without realizing it. Phenomena include ethical fading (the moral dimension recedes from view, often via framing a choice as a "business" or "legal" decision), motivated reasoning, the slippery slope of incremental escalation, and the gap between the "want" self and the "should" self.

Albert Bandura's (1999) theory of moral disengagement catalogues the specific mechanisms by which self-sanction is selectively switched off: moral justification, euphemistic labeling, advantageous comparison, displacement and diffusion of responsibility, disregarding or distorting consequences, and dehumanization/attribution of blame to victims. These mechanisms are visible verbatim in the testimony from Enron, Wells Fargo, and Volkswagen, where employees described "the way things were done" using sanitizing language.

Worked example: a structured decision framework

Most practical models (e.g., the Markkula Center framework, Laura Nash's twelve questions, the "PLUS" filter) operationalize Rest into steps. Applied to a manager pressured to recognize revenue early to hit a quarterly target:

  • Recognize (awareness): Name it — this is potential financial misrepresentation, not an "aggressive accounting" judgment call.
  • Get the facts: What do GAAP and policy actually require? Who is asking, and why?
  • Identify stakeholders: investors relying on the statements, auditors, colleagues, the manager's own integrity.
  • Evaluate through multiple lenses: utilitarian (aggregate harm of misled investors vs. short-term gain), rights/deontological (investors' right to truthful information; the duty not to deceive), virtue (would a person of integrity do this?), justice (who bears the risk if it unravels?).
  • Apply tests: the publicity test (front page of the newspaper), the reversibility/role-reversal test (would I accept this if I were the investor?), and the generalization test (what if everyone did this?).
  • Act and reflect: decline, escalate through proper channels, document, and review the outcome.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Assuming knowledge equals behavior. Teaching theory (component 2) does little if the failure point is awareness or courage.
  • Treating ethics as individual character only. Situation and system — incentives, tone at the top, peer norms — often dominate disposition (the lesson of Milgram and Zimbardo).
  • Confusing legal with ethical. "It's legal" frequently triggers ethical fading; compliance is a floor, not a ceiling.
  • Over-reliance on a single framework. Robust decisions triangulate across consequentialist, deontological, virtue, and justice lenses (see Lessons 2-5).

From individual to organizational

Because intensity, disengagement, and bounded ethicality are partly situational, the strongest interventions are structural: ethical leadership and a speak-up culture (Treviño & Brown, 2004), choice architecture that makes the ethical default easy, decision checklists that force awareness, and protected channels for moral courage. This sets up Lesson 7 (CSR & Stakeholder Theory) and Lesson 8 (Governance), which scale these insights from the individual decision-maker to the organization and its institutions.
